content.text: CSS transitions and animations add motion to interfaces without JavaScript. Transitions respond to state changes (hover, focus, class toggle); animations run independently on a timeline using
. Both animate properties over time with easing functions that make motion feel natural.

attrs.alt: CSS Transitions and Animations: transition shorthand, easing functions, @keyframes anatomy, animation shorthand

content.text: A transition smoothly interpolates a CSS property from one value to another when it changes. Add it to the base state (not the hover state) so both the enter and exit transition are smooth:
property is a shorthand for four sub-properties:
in production. It catches every property change — including layout-triggering ones like
— which can cause unexpected jank. List specific properties explicitly.
Easing controls the rate of change over the transition duration. CSS provides keywords and custom bezier curves:
Not all CSS properties can be animated, and some animate more efficiently than others:
Compositor thread — no layout or paint
Stick to `transform` and `opacity` for smooth 60fps animations.
These run on the compositor thread and never trigger layout or paint. Use

tells the browser to prepare a compositor layer before animation begins, eliminating the first-frame stutter on complex elements. Use it sparingly — only on elements that genuinely animate frequently (modals, drawers, carousels).
defines a named animation sequence with any number of stops:
controls the element's style before and after the animation:
for entrance animations — it prevents the element from flashing at its final state before the animation starts (during the delay), and keeps the element visible after the animation ends.
Hover effects, state changes, UI micro-interactions
Some users experience discomfort or seizures from motion. Always respect the
block is not optional — it is a WCAG 2.3 success criterion. Users who have enabled Reduce Motion in their OS expect their preference to be respected.
Angular provides its own animation system via
that integrates with the change-detection cycle and supports enter/leave transitions for dynamically added/removed DOM elements:
Angular animations work on elements managed by
, and structural directives — they fire when elements enter or leave the DOM, which pure CSS cannot target.
Pure CSS for Component Transitions in Angular
For many cases, pure CSS transitions in the component stylesheet are simpler and faster to load than the Angular animations module:
— layout-triggering properties cause reflow on every frame. Use scale transforms when possible.
Not setting `fill-mode: both` on entrance animations
— the element flashes at its final position during the delay, then jumps back to the start state.
— spinning logos and bouncing elements can cause nausea for vestibular disorder sufferers.
— catches unintended property changes (e.g. a class change that also alters
— more than 2–3 simultaneous animations distract the user. Animate purposefully: guide attention, confirm actions, smooth state changes.

or complex gradients get first-frame jitter without explicit layer promotion.
Staggered entrance animations reveal list items one by one using CSS custom properties for the delay:
Transition Timing: When to Use What
Choosing the right duration and easing is as important as which property to animate:
. Quick feedback that doesn't feel laggy.
— anything slower feels broken to the user.
The View Transitions API (Chrome 111+, Firefox 126+) enables animated page transitions in SPAs with minimal CSS:
in the Angular router automatically triggers view transitions on every navigation. The default cross-fade works out of the box; the CSS above customises it.
CSS transitions animate a property between two states — define them on the base element so both enter and exit are smooth, and prefer
drives independent animations with a full timeline; the
shorthand applies them with duration, easing, delay, iteration, direction, and fill-mode. Use
for entrance animations to prevent flash. Always add a
block that disables or replaces motion — it is a WCAG requirement. In Angular, use pure CSS transitions for hover effects and simple state changes; reach for the
module when you need enter/leave transitions on dynamically added DOM nodes.
